title: Ashoka the Great
excerpt: Mauryan emperor who spread Buddhism and left edicts across the Indian subcontinent.
text: Ashoka the Great Mauryan emperor who spread Buddhism and left edicts across the Indian subcontinent. Relief depicting Ashoka's visit to the Ramagrama stupa, Southern gateway of Sanchi Stupa 1, Madhya Pradesh, India. Photo: Photo Dharma (Flickr) / Wikimedia Commons / CC BY 2.0 Ashoka ruled much of the Indian subcontinent after a violent succession and the Kalinga War, which led him to renounce further conquest and promote non-violence. His rock and pillar edicts record policies on tolerance, welfare, and moral conduct. Ashoka sent missionaries beyond India and helped Buddhism become a major world religion. The relief above, from Sanchi, is among the visual traditions linking Ashoka to Buddhist pilgrimage and the spread of the dharma.

title: The Mauryan Empire
excerpt: India's first large empire, unifying much of the subcontinent under Chandragupta and Ashoka.
text: The Mauryan Empire India's first large empire, unifying much of the subcontinent under Chandragupta and Ashoka. Founded by Chandragupta Maurya with guidance from Chanakya, the Mauryan Empire (c. 322–185 BCE) created a centralized administration and vast road networks. Emperor Ashoka later embraced Buddhism and promoted dharma through edicts carved on pillars and rocks across the realm. The empire's decline followed Ashoka's death and succession disputes.

title: Indus Valley Civilization
excerpt: One of the world's earliest urban cultures, flourishing along the Indus River around 2500 BCE.
text: Indus Valley Civilization One of the world's earliest urban cultures, flourishing along the Indus River around 2500 BCE. The Indus Valley Civilization (also called the Harappan Civilization) developed sophisticated cities such as Harappa and Mohenjo-daro, with planned streets, drainage systems, and standardized weights. Archaeological evidence suggests extensive trade networks and a script that remains undeciphered. The civilization declined around 1900 BCE, possibly due to climate change and shifts in river courses.
